content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 30 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 115r–116v | **Source Images:** DEU_030_page_001.jpg; DEU_030_page_002.jpg; DEU_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** When all these things have happened to you — the blessings and the curses I have set before you — and you take them to heart wherever the LORD your God has scattered you among the nations, **2** and when you and your children return to the LORD your God and obey him with all your heart and with all your soul according to everything I command you today, **3** then the LORD your God will restore your fortunes and have compassion on you and gather you again from all the nations where he scattered you. **4** Even if you have been banished to the most distant land under the heavens, from there the LORD your God will gather you and bring you back. **5** He will bring you to the land that belonged to your ancestors, and you will take possession of it. He will make you more prosperous and numerous than your ancestors.

**6** The LORD your God will circumcise your hearts and the hearts of your descendants, so that you may love him with all your heart and with all your soul, and live. **7** The LORD your God will put all these curses on your enemies who hate and persecute you. **8** You will again obey the LORD and follow all his commands I am giving you today. **9** Then the LORD your God will make you most prosperous in all the work of your hands and in the fruit of your womb, the young of your livestock, and the crops of your land. The LORD will again delight in you and make you prosperous, just as he delighted in your ancestors, **10** if you obey the LORD your God and keep his commands and decrees that are written in this Book of the Instruction and turn to the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul.

**11** Now what I am commanding you today is not too difficult for you or beyond your reach. **12** It is not up in heaven, so that you have to ask, "Who will ascend into heaven to get it and proclaim it to us so we may obey it?" **13** Nor is it beyond the sea, so that you have to ask, "Who will cross the sea to get it and proclaim it to us so we may obey it?" **14** No, the word is very near you; it is in your mouth and in your heart so you may obey it.

**15** See, I set before you today life and prosperity, death and destruction. **16** For I command you today to love the LORD your God, to walk in obedience to him, and to keep his commands, decrees, and laws; then you will live and increase, and the LORD your God will bless you in the land you are entering to possess.

**17** But if your heart turns away and you are not obedient, and if you are drawn away to bow down to other gods and worship them, **18** I declare to you this day that you will certainly be destroyed. You will not live long in the land you are crossing the Jordan to enter and possess.

**19** This day I call the heavens and the earth as witnesses against you that I have set before you life and death, blessings and curses. Now choose life, so that you and your children may live **20** and that you may love the LORD your God, listen to his voice, and hold fast to him. For the LORD is your life, and he will give you many years in the land he swore to give to your ancestors, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "Take them to heart":** The Hebrew va-hashevota el levavecha literally means "you return it to your heart/mind." PNEUMA renders this as "take them to heart" because the action described is interior recognition — the exiles in the nations, surrounded by blessing and curse, come to understand what happened and why.

**Verses 3-5 — Restoration as compassion:** The verb shav shevutcha ("restore your fortunes") is a pun in Hebrew on the root shuv (return/turn). The LORD's restoration is itself a turning — as Israel turns back to God, God turns back toward Israel. PNEUMA renders the sequence as active compassion: gather, bring back, give the land, make prosperous.

**Verse 6 — Circumcised hearts:** This is one of the most theologically dense verses in the Torah. The Lord himself will circumcise (mul) the hearts of the people — the external covenantal sign will become an interior transformation. The result is love: "so that you may love him with all your heart and with all your soul." PNEUMA keeps "circumcise" as the literal and theologically loaded verb rather than softening it. Paul draws on this verse in Romans 2:29.

**Verses 11-14 — The Torah is not inaccessible:** The passage repudiates every excuse for not knowing or obeying the covenant instruction. It is not in heaven (requiring a heavenly mediator), not across the sea (requiring a heroic voyager) — it is already in your mouth and heart. The word is democratically near. PNEUMA renders lo niflet hi ("it is not hidden/beyond reach") as "not too difficult for you or beyond your reach" to capture both the spatial and cognitive senses.

**Verse 14 — "In your mouth and in your heart":** This is the verse Paul quotes in Romans 10:8 when speaking of the accessibility of the gospel message. The proximity of the divine word is the central claim of this passage. PNEUMA keeps "mouth" before "heart" as in the Hebrew, because oral proclamation (Torah reading, teaching, recitation) is how it reaches the heart.

**Verse 15 — Life and prosperity / death and destruction:** The Hebrew ha-chayyim ve-ha-tov ve-ha-mavet ve-ha-ra ("life and good, death and evil") is the four-word summary of the entire covenant choice. PNEUMA renders tov/ra as "prosperity/destruction" in this context because the passage has been specifying concrete historical outcomes (land, children, livestock, exile), not abstract moral states.

**Verse 19 — "Choose life":** U-vacharta ba-chayyim is the imperative the whole speech has been building toward. PNEUMA renders it as a simple, direct command followed by the reason: "so that you and your children may live." The call witnesses (heaven and earth) and the stakes (life or death) have been set out — now the only remaining question is what Israel will choose. The verse functions as the theological summit of the entire deuteronomic address.

**Verse 20 — "The LORD is your life":** Ki hu chayyekha — "For he is your life." PNEUMA renders this as the theological foundation of the "choose life" command: to choose life is not to choose a set of rules but to choose the living God himself, who is the source of life. The chapter closes with the patriarchal names (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) anchoring the covenant promise in deep history.

— PNEUMA
